title: Religious Interpretation of EoEs Conclusion
wikitext:
[]: Religious Interpretation of EoE's Conclusion             Quote Post by Thepocalypse » Wed Jul 01, 2009 7:32 pm First off, you need to know these simple bits of Christian mythology. 1. Adam was the first human. 2. Eve came later. 3. Lilith was Adam's demonic wife. My theory is quite simple, NGE is either a retelling of Genesis or history repeating itself and Genesis reoccurring. Everyone is primordial ooze in the beginning, and from that ooze comes a man. In Genesis this would be Adam forming in God's image, in NGE this is Shinji. Adam/Shinji is alone for some time before a woman comes from the nether, Eve in Genesis and Asuka in NGE. Remember Rei's mention of "The Woman That Does Not Bleed"? This is easily interpreted as an implication that she does not menstruate. Interestingly, neither did Lilith. She was incapable of baring children. Anno has stated that he wanted Rei to come off as a more creepy and distant character, but she was well received in a... well the words ZOMGTEHREI!1! come to mind. This is similar to the manner in which Lilith should be ugly, but is seen as a beauty to all men. The gist of the theory; Not only are Asuka and Shinji Adam and Eve incarnate, Rei is a representation of Lilith, the ever present "alternate love interest" to our Adam. I know this will get me flamed, but if you wanted to, you could incorporate the theory that Asuka was pregnant at the end of EoE into this easily as being the first step in the world's population. It is not integral to the interpretation.

[]:             Quote Post by xfactor30seconds » Wed Jul 01, 2009 8:26 pm The only issue with the is that at the end of EOE it is said that "all living creatures have the power to come back to life and the will to go on living......" Also it is said that if someone has a clear image of themselves and wants to come back even knowing the pain and misery the world holds then reapear. There fore Asuka wouldnt need to be pregnant

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 01, 2009 9:43 pm NGE/EoE is not a retelling of Genesis but an appropriation of many of its themes and concepts grafted onto a new context which highlights a more psychological interpretation ala Jung. EoE's Genesis is not the Biblical Genesis but a metonymic 'new beginning' for its characters and the world; the manifested expression of EoTVs 'change your perception of the world and the world changes' concept. Yes, there are certain particular parallels, but the narrative tells us what to take away and what to leave. Shinji and Asuka don't represent Adam and Eve because we learn in Instrumentality that anyone can come back. Lilith is not 'the' Lilith but merely a name given to her by Seele or, actually, Anno and there is very little association between her and the Bible's apocryphal character (except how she links with The Black Moon and, perhaps, can be seen as the real outcast on Earth). There are some other Biblical concepts that relate, such as the a kind of 2nd 'fall of man' after attaining oneness with God (in Instrumentality/Eden/Paradise), but, again, the differences help to highlight as many themes as the similarities, such as this oneness being a return to a collective womb of Lilith, which is another metonym for NGE's theme of beginnings and the duality of collectivism VS individuality, etc. I really need to put all of this is an exhaustive essay one day.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Wed Jul 01, 2009 10:29 pm Something Eva Yojimbo has taught me (yes, you just above me): although things from religion can be used to help interpret scenes from evangelion, particularly with respect to the symbolism, the interpretation that emerges should not be religious itself as that is not what evangelion was meant to be. As such, although your idea that it was a retelling of genesis as stated in bibles is most likely flawed, some of the things you've brought up such as the representation of Rei with respect to lilith, may be good to look more into. (i.e. Asuka was there for quite a while at the end there before that Rei apparition shows up, spurring Shinji to do his choking thing for some reason)

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Thu Jul 02, 2009 1:41 am Evangelion isn't really meant to be a direct allegory of any particular bible story. There are some themes that are carried over (one that I've often noticed is the similarities between SEELE and the commonly perceived notion of a one-world government in Revelation, and the similarities between Third Impact and the rapture), but I think that the most genuine reflection of a biblical theme, even if it wasn't intentional, is Yui Ikari as a Christ figure. Out of love she gets "crucified" to save her son (and by extension, humanity), and then ascends, promising Shinji that they'll be re-united after the end of days (which seems to suggest that Yui believes in an afterlife / heaven other than what SEELE does). It's remarkably similar to Christ's crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension. If Yui is Christ, then similarly, Shinji could be seen as a sinner whom Christ/Yui seeks to save.

[]: Re: Religious Interpretation of EoE's Conclusion             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jul 02, 2009 1:48 am Thepocalypse wrote: First off, you need to know these simple bits of Christian mythology.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 02, 2009 2:18 am Timstuff wrote: Yui Ikari as a Christ figure. Out of love she gets "crucified" to save her son (and by extension, humanity), and then ascends, promising Shinji that they'll be re-united after the end of days (which seems to suggest that Yui believes in an afterlife / heaven other than what SEELE does).

[]:             Quote Post by FallenTabris » Thu Jul 02, 2009 6:27 am Where people have tried to take this literal tack, we end up with things like the suggestion that at the very end of EoE, we see Adam (Shinji) try to kill Lilith (Asuka, the active and dominant) in order to let the waiting Eve (Rei, the passive and submissive) into the world.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jul 02, 2009 6:42 am FallenTabris wrote: wat

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Thu Jul 02, 2009 10:09 am Let's remember that religions are themselves "burrowing" from what came before. The Jews stole angels from the Zoroastrians. Christianity stole concepts of souls and bodies from Neo-Platonism. You could argue that the idea of a demonic regent of hell working in sincere opposition to God was stolen from Mani's teachings. Islam stole its "demons" from the Arab tribal faiths. Lilith isn't part of canonical Judaism or Christianity, but from later myths based on other faiths, as has been noted here. If anything, Evangelion uses the strange and wondrous mythology of Judeo-Christianity to blanket fundamentally Asian ideas about the nature of the soul and the unity of all things. The concept of the "world soul" tells us that we are shells surrounding mere extensions of the world soul, and when we die, our shells (bodies) break, releasing that extension of soul, allowing it to return to its origin. "Will you become one with me?" is a central question here. When we die (or transcend) we become one with everything. When we ask where "God" can be found in Evangelion, it is safe to assume everything is God... or at least... everything with a soul. Of course, it is all allegory and metaphor, so trying to come up with an exact system of influence or metaphysics... haha. X_x Besides, the biggest problem is this: Asuka comes from Lilith, meaning she cannot be Eve. Only something that comes from Adam can be Eve. And then explain all the crosses? Pretty much arbitrary. Let's not forget that Shinji also comes from Lilith, and that Adam is somebody else. The names and terms themselves are for colour. Also, Lilith is supposed to be the embodiment of female evil, being a woman who has no use for a man (will not accept the missionary position, will not make babies, will not bake pies). I'm really just "thinking out loud" here, but I'm guessing the chief Biblical reference (that doesn't get twisted and mashed up) would be the idea that wisdom and life combined grant you immortality like God. Hence, immortality and efforts to become like god are evil things. Bad Seele! The rest is very... Asian.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Jul 02, 2009 10:17 am I may be wrong, but I always thought EoE was a retelling of the End Times in the Biblical book of Revelation, where God returns everything back to the way it was before sin entered the world through Adam. And since the theory of evolution was fused with that Biblical account in NGE, the world as it used to be was interpreted as primordial ooze rather that the Biblical account of a perfect world without death. Me being a Christian and knowing much about scripture, that's how I interpreted it.

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Thu Jul 02, 2009 11:47 am @Mr. Tines: Totally agree. @oOoOoOo: Mostly agree very much - all except the notion that God can be "safely assumed" to be everything; that's rather interpretive and based on a particular context (which I might personally agree with, but that is not overt in NGE). We cannot safely assume there is any notion of a Supreme Being in NGE - but we can exercise some theories, if we first define the role and nature of an NGE "God." But it would be an "Asian" view (Eastern as opposed to Western). @FreakyFilmFan4ever: You may wish to invest in some comparative religion study. You may find a very wide ranging view of the Book of Revelations, even among the very different segments of Christianity.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jul 02, 2009 12:59 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: a retelling of the End Times in the Biblical book of Revelation, where God returns everything back to the way it was

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Thu Jul 02, 2009 2:13 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: I may be wrong, but I always thought EoE was a retelling of the End Times in the Biblical book of Revelation, where God returns everything back to the way it was before sin entered the world through Adam.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jul 02, 2009 2:31 pm TL/DR: bring your spiritual and metaphysical questions with you when watching NGE, but don't try to make it fit any particular worldview. The results of such shoehorning will not be pretty. Also, the judeo-christian influences were chosen specifically to be mysterious to the intended audience. If it were created for a western audience we might have called the first seeds Shiva and Vishnu instead, the evas would be Brahmans, the angels as Devas, etc.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Jul 02, 2009 3:44 pm Mr. Tines wrote: FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: a retelling of the End Times in the Biblical book of Revelation, where God returns everything back to the way it was

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Thu Jul 02, 2009 3:46 pm Yay, another one of these threads...

[]:             Quote Post by Timstuff » Thu Jul 02, 2009 3:54 pm Mr. Tines wrote: FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: a retelling of the End Times in the Biblical book of Revelation, where God returns everything back to the way it was

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Thu Jul 02, 2009 4:21 pm ZapalacX wrote: Yay, another one of these threads...
